articles.title: People prefer negotiating with women over men, study suggests
articles.summary: New research shows that people walk away from deals more satisfied when their counterpart is a woman. Women achieve the exact same financial results as men but excel at building relationships, leading to more future business opportunities.
articles.aiSummary: New research shows that people walk away from deals more satisfied when their counterpart is a woman. Women achieve the exact same financial results as men but excel at building relationships, leading to more future business opportunities.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…an Airline Pilot
articles.summary: Shooting cat-three landings in the fog, using the “voice of God” on unruly passengers, and declaring Mayday after an engine fire with Paul Drusch, a commercial airline pilot. Why does the pilot’s paycheck start with the parking brake? And what does “sterile cockpit” mean? (Spoiler: it doesn’t mean “clean.”) [buzzsprout episode='19294742' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: What It’s Like to Be…a Clinical Ethicist
articles.summary: Untangling who gets to speak for a dying patient, weighing a treatment's benefits against its burdens, and searching for clarity in the grayest corners of healthcare with Esther Berkowitz, a clinical ethicist. What is the "dignity of risk"? And how do you know which "version" of a person to trust? [buzzsprout episode='19262736' player='true'] View the episode transcript About What It's Like to Be... articles.title: Eight weeks of guided slow breathing alters stress responses in veterans
articles.summary: A daily routine of device-guided slow breathing helps veterans with trauma disorders dial back their physical fight-or-flight response to sudden mental stress, suggesting a new path for protecting the cardiovascular health of vulnerable patients without relying on medications.
articles.aiSummary: A daily routine of device-guided slow breathing helps veterans with trauma disorders dial back their physical fight-or-flight response to sudden mental stress, suggesting a new path for protecting the cardiovascular health of vulnerable patients without relying on medications.

articles.title: How people interpret life milestones is tied to how their personalities develop
articles.summary: Entering a relationship or changing jobs often coincides with personality changes. However, new research shows these associations are highly individualized, relying on a person's age, gender, and subjective perception.
articles.aiSummary: Entering a relationship or changing jobs often coincides with personality changes. However, new research shows these associations are highly individualized, relying on a person's age, gender, and subjective perception.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Entering a relationship or changing jobs often coincides with personality changes. However, new research shows these associations are highly individualized, relying on a person's age, gender, and subjective perception.

articles.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
articles.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.aiSummary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

articles.title: Baby teeth reveal how early metal exposures shape the adolescent brain
articles.summary: Using baby teeth to reconstruct weekly chemical exposures, scientists found that the specific timing of early metal absorption is associated with differences in the developing brain and later adolescent behavior.
articles.aiSummary: Using baby teeth to reconstruct weekly chemical exposures, scientists found that the specific timing of early metal absorption is associated with differences in the developing brain and later adolescent behavior.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Using baby teeth to reconstruct weekly chemical exposures, scientists found that the specific timing of early metal absorption is associated with differences in the developing brain and later adolescent behavior.

articles.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—similar to what is seen in ADHD—but regained normal focus as soon as the neurons were reactivated.

articles.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
articles.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

articles.title: Love and money both matter for health, but they don’t replace each other
articles.summary: A new study reveals that a happy romantic relationship and a sense of financial security are independently linked to better physical and mental health. The findings suggest that both domains are important, but neither replaces the other.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals that a happy romantic relationship and a sense of financial security are independently linked to better physical and mental health. The findings suggest that both domains are important, but neither replaces the other.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study reveals that a happy romantic relationship and a sense of financial security are independently linked to better physical and mental health. The findings suggest that both domains are important, but neither replaces the other.

articles.title: Men and women show different psychological links between the “fit ideal” and risky behaviors
articles.summary: The push for a perfectly toned physique takes a toll on mental health. A new study reveals that while both men and women feel the pressure of the modern fitness ideal, the psychological paths to disordered eating differ completely.
articles.aiSummary: The push for a perfectly toned physique takes a toll on mental health. A new study reveals that while both men and women feel the pressure of the modern fitness ideal, the psychological paths to disordered eating differ completely.

articles.title: Parents invest differently in daughters and sons, study finds
articles.summary: A new study published in Human Nature reports that parents do not simply invest more in daughters or sons overall, rather, their investment differs by domain, with mothers, fathers, daughters, and sons showing distinct patterns.
articles.aiSummary: A new study published in Human Nature reports that parents do not simply invest more in daughters or sons overall, rather, their investment differs by domain, with mothers, fathers, daughters, and sons showing distinct patterns.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study published in Human Nature reports that parents do not simply invest more in daughters or sons overall, rather, their investment differs by domain, with mothers, fathers, daughters, and sons showing distinct patterns.

articles.title: Scientists discover deep brain stimulation physically reshapes the brain’s information superhighway
articles.summary: Deep brain stimulation offers hope for severe depression, but how it works has remained a mystery. A new study reveals the therapy actually physically reshapes the brain’s white matter pathways, explaining its long-lasting mood-boosting effects.
articles.aiSummary: Deep brain stimulation offers hope for severe depression, but how it works has remained a mystery. A new study reveals the therapy actually physically reshapes the brain’s white matter pathways, explaining its long-lasting mood-boosting effects.

articles.title: Prenatal exposure to air pollution is linked to increased attention issues in children
articles.summary: A new study shows that pregnant women exposed to higher levels of air pollution may have children who exhibit more attention-related symptoms in school. However, this exposure is not associated with higher rates of formal clinical ADHD diagnoses.
articles.aiSummary: A new study shows that pregnant women exposed to higher levels of air pollution may have children who exhibit more attention-related symptoms in school. However, this exposure is not associated with higher rates of formal clinical ADHD diagnoses.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study shows that pregnant women exposed to higher levels of air pollution may have children who exhibit more attention-related symptoms in school. However, this exposure is not associated with higher rates of formal clinical ADHD diagnoses.

articles.title: A balanced diet of video games is associated with greater stoicism and less isolation
articles.summary: Are video game habits connected to isolation? A new study reveals that adults who play open-world and accessible single-player games report significantly lower feelings of loneliness, a trend that is closely associated with higher stoic attitudes.
articles.aiSummary: Are video game habits connected to isolation? A new study reveals that adults who play open-world and accessible single-player games report significantly lower feelings of loneliness, a trend that is closely associated with higher stoic attitudes.

articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T

articles.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
articles.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.

articles.title: Competitive students use ChatGPT to memorize trivia instead of actually learning
articles.summary: When students try to outsmart their peers, their academic performance suffers. A new psychology study reveals that pushing learners to compete leads them to ask ChatGPT superficial questions, increasing their anxiety and severely hindering true knowledge acquisition.
articles.aiSummary: When students try to outsmart their peers, their academic performance suffers. A new psychology study reveals that pushing learners to compete leads them to ask ChatGPT superficial questions, increasing their anxiety and severely hindering true knowledge acquisition.

articles.title: Neural Architecture Search for Generative Adversarial Networks: A Comprehensive Review and Critical Analysis
articles.summary: arXiv:2606.26169v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a pivotal technique in optimizing the design of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), automating the search for effective architectures while addressing the challenges inherent in manual design. This paper provides a comprehensive review of NAS methods applied to GANs, categorizing and comparing various approaches based on criteria such as search strategies, evaluation metrics, and performance outcomes. The review highlights the benefits of NAS in improving GAN performance, stability, and efficien
